name: Interest on Reserves
abstract: This article critically examines the Congressional proposal, notably from Senator Ted Cruz, to cease paying interest on bank reserves (IOR). The author refutes the stated reasons for the proposal—saving government money and preventing payments to foreign banks—by likening IOR to other forms of government debt and illustrating the severe, unfeasible consequences of such a policy. It explores two potential Fed responses: a massive undoing of quantitative easing (QE), which might be financially impossible given the Fed's portfolio losses, or financial repression, forcing banks to hold interest-free reserves, effectively taxing them and undermining the traditional banking system. The piece clarifies misconceptions about Fed profits and explains that eliminating IOR, especially while maintaining large reserve quantities, is akin to forcing the Fed's interest rate target to zero, which is generally inflationary. The author concludes by advocating for ample, interest-paying reserves as a beneficial innovation that enhances financial markets and saves the government money, contrasting it with a
